title: Banana plant fibre papermaking
subtitle: Preparing and pulling sheets from abaca-free banana stem fibre
excerpt: Banana stems contain long, strong fibres that produce a distinctive paper: slightly translucent, with a warm cream tone and a natural fibre texture. Unlike commercial abaca (which comes from the closely related Musa textilis), fresh banana plant stems from the garden or greengrocer are processed at home into usable pulp.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923, public domain via Internet Archive), on the use of bast fibres from tropical plants in Asian papermaking traditions. The banana fibre preparation method follows the bast-fibre processing principles Hunter documents for kozo and related fibres.

glossaryTerms.definition: Long plant fibres extracted from the inner bark or stem of a plant, such as kozo, flax, hemp, and banana. Bast fibres are long and strong, producing paper with good tear resistance.
The process of soaking plant stems in water to soften the outer tissues and make the bast fibre easier to separate.
Plant fibre that has been mechanically beaten to break down the cell walls and create a slurry of individual fibres that bond together when wet and dry into a sheet.

content.text: The banana plant (Musa acuminata and its close relatives) stores its structural fibres in the pseudostem, the thick cylindrical leaf-base structure that looks like a trunk. After the fruit has been harvested, the stem is cut down and the fibrous inner layers are available. The
from the inner sheaths are long, cream-coloured, and strong. A well-beaten banana fibre pulp produces sheets with a characteristic translucency and a lightly textured surface.

attrs.heading: Blender safety
attrs.body: Use a dedicated blender for pulp work, separate from kitchen appliances. Keep the blender unplugged when adding fibre or rinsing. Drain the vat before reaching in.

items.name: Fresh banana pseudostem sections
items.notes: The inner leaf sheaths from a freshly harvested or purchased banana stem. The outermost brown layers are discarded; the pale inner sheaths are the source of fibre.

items.name: Papermaking blender (dedicated)
items.notes: For beating the fibre into pulp.

items.name: Papermaking vat
items.notes: A wide, low-sided tub large enough to hold the mould and deckle flat.

items.name: Press boards
items.notes: For pressing the post of couched sheets.

content.text: Peel the inner sheaths from the pseudostem and cut them into 10 cm lengths. Submerge in cold water and leave to

for 24 hours, which softens the outer tissue and makes the fibres easier to separate. After retting, pull apart the fibrous layers with your hands; the long parallel fibres come away in strips. Rinse thoroughly under running water. Discard the soft outer tissue; keep only the fibrous strips.

Banana fibre is tough enough to resist cold-water beating alone. Cook the fibre strips in a large pot with water for 60 to 90 minutes at a rolling simmer. This breaks down the lignin bonding between the individual plant cells and makes the fibre separate more easily in the blender. Do not add wood ash or caustic soda; banana fibre is amenable to plain-water cooking at home scale. Rinse the cooked fibre thoroughly.

Place a small handful of cooked fibre in the blender with 500 ml of water. Pulse five times, then blend at medium speed for 30 seconds. Examine the result: well-beaten banana fibre should be a smooth slurry of individual fibres with no visible clumps. If clumps remain, blend for another 30 seconds. The
is added to the vat one blender-load at a time until the vat water reaches the right consistency: approximately 2% fibre by volume.

Pull and couch banana fibre sheets using the standard nagashizuki (Western) pulling method. The fibre drains slightly more slowly than cotton linter because the fibres are longer; wait an extra 10 seconds on the mould before couching. Allow the couched sheets to press overnight under weight before peeling them from the couching cloth for air-drying. Banana fibre sheets are particularly prone to cockling as they dry; dry them on a smooth board with gentle heat from a fan (not direct heat) for the flattest result.

items.symptom: The sheet pulls unevenly with thick and thin patches
items.cause: The fibre was not beaten long enough and the pulp is not evenly dispersed.
items.fix: Stir the vat thoroughly before each pull. Blend the fibre for longer on the next batch.

items.symptom: The sheets cockle badly after drying
items.cause: The sheets dried too quickly on one side.
items.fix: Press under boards for longer before peeling. Fan-dry face-up on a smooth surface or board-dry on a sealed ply board.
